title: An Argument About Inheritance
summary: Two adult siblings disagree about what to do with money they have unexpectedly inherited from their grandmother, revealing deeper tensions about fairness, family history, and each person's needs.

body.speaker: Sister
body.text: I think we should talk about Gran's money properly. We've been avoiding it for weeks, and it's starting to feel uncomfortable.

body.speaker: Brother
body.text: I know. I've been putting it off too. The will said we split it equally, but that doesn't necessarily mean we have to use it in the same way or at the same time.

body.speaker: Sister
body.text: That's what I wanted to say, actually. I was thinking I might put most of my half into the house. We've needed a new roof for two years, and if we wait any longer, the cost will only go up.

body.speaker: Brother
body.text: That makes sense for you. My situation is different. I've been thinking I might use some of mine to pay off the credit card debt. I know you probably think that's irresponsible, but the interest has been eating into everything.

body.speaker: Sister
body.text: I don't think it's irresponsible at all. You've had a really hard couple of years with the business not going well. Gran would have understood that.

body.speaker: Brother
body.text: Maybe. I'm still a bit uncomfortable about it, honestly. It feels like I'm using money that was meant to be a gift as something to dig myself out of a hole.

body.speaker: Sister
body.text: But that's what it's for, isn't it? She left it to us because she wanted to help us, not because she had a specific idea of what we should spend it on.

body.speaker: Brother
body.text: I suppose. The part I'm struggling with is whether we should be setting some aside for Mum. She's not mentioned in the will, but Gran did rely on her a lot in the last few years. It doesn't feel entirely fair to me.

body.speaker: Sister
body.text: I hear you, and I thought about that too. But Gran chose not to include Mum in the will, and that was her decision to make. If we start giving portions to people who weren't named, we could end up with half the extended family expecting something.

body.speaker: Brother
body.text: That's a bit cynical, isn't it? I'm talking about Mum, not the extended family. She gave up three years of weekends to look after Gran.

body.speaker: Sister
body.text: I know she did, and I'm not dismissing that. But she also said herself that she did it because she wanted to, not because she expected anything in return. If we give her money out of our inheritance, it changes the nature of what she did.

body.speaker: Brother
body.text: I hadn't thought of it that way. I still feel like we should do something, even if it's not money. Maybe take her on a proper holiday, the three of us. Gran would have liked that.

body.speaker: Sister
body.text: I'd be happy to do that. That seems different from redirecting part of the inheritance. It's coming from us, as a choice.

body.speaker: Brother
body.text: Agreed. There's one more thing I wanted to raise. Gran's piano is still at the house. Neither of us plays, but I feel like it should go to someone who will actually use it. There's a music school down the road that takes donated instruments.

body.speaker: Sister
body.text: That's a lovely idea. Though it might be worth checking whether it has any value first. Some older pianos are worth restoring. We shouldn't give it away and then find out it was worth something.

body.speaker: Brother
body.text: Fair point. I'll ask around before we decide. I suppose I just want to make sure that the things she valued don't end up in a skip.

body.speaker: Sister
body.text: I feel the same. She was very careful with everything she owned. The least we can do is be careful with what she's left behind.

annotations.meaningVi: trì hoãn, tránh né (làm việc gì đó)
annotations.exampleEn: I've been putting it off for weeks but we need to talk.
The will said we should split it equally.

annotations.meaningVi: ăn mòn, làm tiêu hao dần (tiền bạc, thời gian)
annotations.exampleEn: The credit card interest has been eating into everything.

annotations.phrase: dig myself out of a hole
annotations.meaningVi: thoát khỏi tình huống tệ hại, gỡ rối
annotations.exampleEn: It feels like I'm using it to dig myself out of a hole.

annotations.meaningVi: để dành, giữ lại một phần
annotations.exampleEn: Should we be setting some aside for Mum?
Gran did rely on Mum a lot in the last few years.

annotations.meaningVi: rốt cuộc có, kết thúc với
annotations.exampleEn: We could end up with half the extended family expecting something.

annotations.meaningVi: phủ nhận điều đó, gạt sang một bên
annotations.exampleEn: I'm not dismissing what Mum did — I know she worked hard.
Giving her money changes the nature of what she did.

annotations.meaningVi: chuyển hướng một phần thừa kế
annotations.exampleEn: That seems different from redirecting part of the inheritance.
There's a music school that takes donated instruments.
Some older pianos are worth restoring.

annotations.meaningVi: bị vứt vào thùng rác (container lớn), bị bỏ đi
annotations.exampleEn: I don't want her things to end up in a skip.
There's one more thing I wanted to raise.

annotations.meaningVi: hoài nghi, thờ ơ, không tin tưởng vào động cơ của người khác
annotations.exampleEn: That's a bit cynical — I'm talking about Mum, not everyone.

annotations.meaningVi: để đổi lại, để được đền đáp
annotations.exampleEn: She did it because she wanted to, not because she expected anything in return.

grammarNotes.title: Modals of Speculation and Possibility
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hội thoại dùng nhiều động từ khuyết thiếu để suy đoán và diễn đạt khả năng: 'Gran would have understood that', 'She would have liked that', 'we could end up with half the extended family expecting something', 'it might be worth checking', 'it should go to someone who will actually use it'. Cấu trúc: would have + V3 (suy đoán về quá khứ); could/might + V nguyên (khả năng trong tương lai).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses modals extensively for speculation and possibility: 'Gran would have understood', 'we could end up with...', 'it might be worth checking', 'it should go to someone who will use it'. Form: would have + past participle (speculating about past); could/might + infinitive (future possibility); should + infinitive (obligation or recommendation).

grammarNotes.title: Reported Speech and Indirect Thoughts
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Các nhân vật tường thuật điều người khác đã nói và điều họ đã nghĩ: 'she also said herself that she did it because she wanted to', 'I feel like it should go to someone', 'It feels like I'm using money... to dig myself out of a hole'. Trong lời nói thông thường, 'I feel like / It feels like' thường theo sau mệnh đề bổ ngữ (that-clause), đôi khi bỏ 'that': 'I feel like (that) it should...'. Tường thuật chuyển thì khi diễn đạt điều người khác nói trước đó.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Characters report what others said and what they themselves thought: 'she also said herself that she did it because she wanted to', 'It feels like I'm using money to dig myself out of a hole'. In informal speech, 'I feel like' often precedes a that-clause with 'that' omitted. Reported speech shifts tense: 'she said she did it because she wanted to' (not 'she wants').

translationVi:
Chị: Tôi nghĩ chúng ta nên nói chuyện thật sự về số tiền của bà. Chúng ta đã tránh né điều này suốt nhiều tuần, và nó bắt đầu khiến tôi khó chịu.

Anh: Tôi biết. Tôi cũng đã trì hoãn. Tờ di chúc nói chúng ta chia đều, nhưng điều đó không nhất thiết có nghĩa là chúng ta phải sử dụng nó theo cách giống nhau hoặc vào cùng một thời điểm.

Chị: Đó thực ra là điều tôi muốn nói. Tôi đang nghĩ tôi có thể dùng phần lớn nửa của mình để đầu tư cho ngôi nhà. Chúng ta cần mái nhà mới hai năm nay rồi, và nếu đợi thêm, chi phí sẽ chỉ tăng thêm.

Anh: Điều đó có lý với em. Tình huống của anh thì khác. Anh đang nghĩ có thể dùng một phần để trả nợ thẻ tín dụng. Anh biết em có thể nghĩ điều đó thiếu trách nhiệm, nhưng tiền lãi đã ăn mòn vào mọi thứ.

Chị: Tôi không nghĩ nó thiếu trách nhiệm chút nào. Anh đã có hai năm thực sự khó khăn khi kinh doanh không tốt. Bà nội sẽ hiểu điều đó.

Anh: Có thể. Thành thật mà nói anh vẫn cảm thấy hơi khó chịu về điều đó. Cảm giác như mình đang dùng số tiền được cho là quà để thoát khỏi tình huống khó khăn.

Chị: Nhưng đó là mục đích của nó phải không? Bà để lại cho chúng ta vì bà muốn giúp chúng ta, không phải vì bà có ý tưởng cụ thể về việc chúng ta nên tiêu nó vào gì.

Anh: Tôi đoán vậy. Phần tôi đang vật lộn là liệu chúng ta có nên để dành một phần cho Mẹ không. Mẹ không được đề cập trong di chúc, nhưng bà nội đã dựa vào Mẹ rất nhiều trong vài năm qua. Điều đó không hoàn toàn công bằng với tôi.

Chị: Tôi nghe anh, và tôi cũng đã nghĩ đến điều đó. Nhưng bà nội đã chọn không đưa Mẹ vào di chúc, và đó là quyết định của bà. Nếu chúng ta bắt đầu cho những người không được nêu tên, chúng ta có thể rốt cuộc với một nửa đại gia đình đang trông đợi điều gì đó.

Anh: Điều đó hơi hoài nghi phải không? Tôi đang nói về Mẹ, không phải đại gia đình. Cô ấy đã bỏ ba năm cuối tuần để chăm sóc bà.

Chị: Tôi biết cô ấy đã làm, và tôi không phủ nhận điều đó. Nhưng cô ấy cũng tự nói rằng cô ấy làm vì muốn, không phải vì mong đợi bất cứ điều gì để đổi lại. Nếu chúng ta cho cô ấy tiền từ thừa kế, nó thay đổi bản chất của những gì cô ấy đã làm.

Anh: Tôi chưa nghĩ theo cách đó. Tôi vẫn cảm thấy chúng ta nên làm gì đó, dù không phải tiền. Có lẽ đưa cô ấy đi nghỉ thật sự, ba người chúng ta. Bà sẽ thích điều đó.

Chị: Tôi sẽ rất vui khi làm vậy. Điều đó có vẻ khác với việc chuyển hướng một phần thừa kế. Nó đến từ chúng ta, như một sự lựa chọn.

Anh: Đồng ý. Còn một điều nữa tôi muốn nêu. Cây đàn piano của bà vẫn còn ở nhà. Không ai trong chúng ta chơi đàn, nhưng tôi cảm thấy nó nên được trao cho ai đó sẽ thực sự sử dụng. Có một trường âm nhạc gần đó nhận nhạc cụ quyên tặng.

Chị: Đó là ý tưởng hay. Mặc dù có lẽ đáng để kiểm tra xem nó có giá trị gì không trước. Một số đàn piano cũ đáng được phục hồi. Chúng ta không nên tặng đi rồi sau đó phát hiện ra nó đáng giá.

Anh: Điểm hay đấy. Tôi sẽ hỏi xung quanh trước khi quyết định. Tôi chỉ muốn chắc chắn rằng những thứ bà trân trọng không bị vứt vào thùng rác.

Chị: Tôi cũng cảm thấy vậy. Bà rất cẩn thận với mọi thứ bà sở hữu. Điều ít nhất chúng ta có thể làm là cẩn thận với những gì bà để lại.

questions.prompt: Why have the siblings been avoiding talking about the inheritance?
options: They disagree about the terms of the will
They are both too busy with their own lives
They have been putting it off because it feels uncomfortable
They are waiting for a lawyer to contact them
questions.explanation: The sister says 'We've been avoiding it for weeks, and it's starting to feel uncomfortable.' The brother confirms he has been 'putting it off too.'
questions.hint: Discomfort, not disagreement or busyness, is the reason.

questions.prompt: What does the sister plan to do with her half of the inheritance?
options: Pay off her credit card debt
Invest it in the stock market
Put most of it into fixing the house, specifically the roof
questions.explanation: The sister says 'I was thinking I might put most of my half into the house. We've needed a new roof for two years.'
questions.hint: A specific part of the house is mentioned.

questions.prompt: Why does the brother feel uncomfortable about using the inheritance to pay debts?
options: He thinks it is illegal to use inherited money for debts
He feels as though he is using a gift to dig himself out of a hole
He is worried the sister will disapprove
He thinks they should invest the money instead
questions.explanation: The brother says 'It feels like I'm using money that was meant to be a gift as something to dig myself out of a hole.'
questions.hint: His discomfort is about the emotional meaning of using the gift this way.

questions.prompt: What does the brother think should be done for their mother?
options: She should be given a formal share of the inheritance
They should take her on a proper holiday as a gesture
They should put her name in the legal documents
They should give her the piano as a gift
questions.explanation: The brother suggests they 'take her on a proper holiday, the three of us.'
questions.hint: His suggestion is an experience, not money.

questions.prompt: Why does the sister argue against giving their mother money from the inheritance?
options: Their mother already has enough money
Their grandmother would not have wanted it
Their grandmother chose not to include their mother in the will, and giving her money changes the nature of what she did
It would create legal problems with the estate
questions.explanation: The sister says 'Gran chose not to include Mum in the will, and that was her decision to make' and 'If we give her money out of our inheritance, it changes the nature of what she did.'
questions.hint: Two reasons are given — one about respecting the will, one about meaning.

questions.prompt: What does the brother say their mother gave up for their grandmother?
questions.explanation: The brother says 'She gave up three years of weekends to look after Gran.'
questions.hint: A specific number and a specific time period are both mentioned.

questions.prompt: Why does the sister say giving the mother money would change the nature of her actions?
options: Because their mother expects to be paid back
Because their mother said she helped voluntarily and not for reward, and money would turn care into a transaction
Because their mother donated her time to charity, not family
Because it would reduce the amount the siblings receive
questions.explanation: The sister says 'she also said herself that she did it because she wanted to, not because she expected anything in return. If we give her money... it changes the nature of what she did.'
questions.hint: The key contrast is between genuine care and paid service.

questions.prompt: What does the brother propose to do with their grandmother's piano?
options: Sell it and share the money
Keep it in the family home as a memory
Donate it to a music school that accepts donated instruments
Send it to a specialist auctioneer
questions.explanation: The brother says 'There's a music school down the road that takes donated instruments.'
questions.hint: His suggestion is charitable and specific.

questions.prompt: What caution does the sister raise about the piano?
options: It might be too large to move
Their grandmother might have wanted a specific person to have it
Some older pianos are worth restoring and it might have financial value
The music school may not want it
questions.explanation: The sister says 'it might be worth checking whether it has any value first. Some older pianos are worth restoring.'
questions.hint: Her caution is practical and financial.

questions.prompt: What is the brother's main concern about the piano and their grandmother's belongings?
options: He wants to make money from them
He does not want things she valued to end up in a skip
He wants them to be kept together in one place
He is worried about the cost of clearing the house
questions.explanation: The brother says 'I just want to make sure that the things she valued don't end up in a skip.'
questions.hint: 'A skip' is a large waste container — he's worried about things being thrown away.

questions.prompt: How does the sister describe her grandmother's relationship with her belongings?
options: She was very careful with everything she owned
She wanted everything given to charity
She never showed interest in her possessions
questions.explanation: The sister says 'She was very careful with everything she owned.'
questions.hint: A simple but meaningful description closes the conversation.

questions.prompt: On what point do the siblings agree by the end of the conversation?
options: They will give half of the inheritance to their mother
They will both invest their money in property
They will take their mother on a holiday and check the piano's value before donating it
They will donate everything to charity as their grandmother would have wished
questions.explanation: They agree to take their mother on a holiday as a gesture, and the brother will check the piano's value before donating it.
questions.hint: Two separate practical decisions are reached by the end.

cloze.template:
Sister: I think we should talk about Gran's money properly. We've been avoiding it for weeks, and it's starting to feel uncomfortable.
Brother: The will said we {{b1}} it equally, but that doesn't mean we have to use it in the same way.
Sister: I was thinking I might put most of my half into the house. We've needed a new roof for two years, and if we wait any longer, the cost will only go {{b2}}.
Brother: My situation is different. I've been thinking I might pay off the credit card debt. The interest has been {{b3}} into everything.
Sister: You've had a really hard couple of years. Gran would have understood that.
Brother: It feels like I'm using money that was meant to be a gift as something to dig myself out of a {{b4}}.
Sister: She left it to us because she wanted to help us, not because she had a specific idea of what we should spend it on.
Brother: The part I'm struggling with is whether we should be setting some {{b5}} for Mum. It doesn't feel entirely fair to me.
Sister: Gran chose not to include Mum in the will, and that was her decision to make. If we start giving portions to people who weren't named, we could {{b6}} up with half the extended family expecting something.
Brother: I'm talking about Mum, not the extended family. She gave up three years of weekends to look after Gran.
Sister: She also said herself that she did it because she wanted to, not because she expected anything {{b7}}. If we give her money out of our inheritance, it changes the {{b8}} of what she did.
Brother: Maybe take her on a proper holiday, the three of us. Gran would have liked that.
Sister: That seems different from {{b9}} part of the inheritance. It's coming from us, as a choice.
Brother: Gran's piano is still at the house. There's a music school down the road that takes {{b10}} instruments. But we should check if it has value first.

blanks.explanation: 'Split it equally' is the exact phrase — to split means to divide into parts.
'Only go up' means costs will increase — a common collocation with rising prices.
'Eating into' is the exact phrasal verb meaning to gradually reduce or consume a resource.
'Dig myself out of a hole' is a fixed idiom meaning to resolve a difficult situation.
'Setting some aside' is the exact phrasal verb meaning to reserve or keep something separately.
'Could end up with' is the exact phrasal verb — to end up means to arrive at an unintended outcome.
'Anything in return' is the exact fixed phrase meaning as compensation or exchange.
'Changes the nature of' is the exact phrase — nature here refers to the essential character of the action.
'Redirecting part of the inheritance' is the exact phrase — to redirect means to change the destination of something.
'Donated instruments' is the exact phrase — donated specifically means given voluntarily without payment.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The sister argues that giving their mother money 'changes the nature' of what she did by turning an act of love into a transaction. Do you agree that financial reward changes the meaning of caring actions? Can something be both heartfelt and financially acknowledged at the same time?
